## Findings

### 1. SSRF — OOB DNS Callback (Info)
**Target:** `www.mitgestalten.wien.gv.at`
**Port:** 80 | **URL:** `http://www.mitgestalten.wien.gv.at`
**Template:** `request-based-interaction` (generic/oast)
**Matcher:** `dns`
**Severity:** info

The target performed an outbound DNS lookup to `d70Tsj3IIoHN55052i4grzGPQoPFmp3AZ.OaSt.fun` — nuclei's OAST server. This indicates the server can be induced to make outbound network requests (DNS or HTTP). Further exploitation would require demonstrating actual data exfiltration or service interaction.

**Actionable next step:** Probe `www.mitgestalten.wien.gv.at` with a collaborator URL to confirm blind SSRF (e.g., request to your collaborator server).

---

### 2. CORS Misconfiguration (Info)
**Target:** `https://hyzdok.magwien.gv.at`
**Port:** 443 | **URL:** `https://hyzdok.magwien.gv.at`
**Matcher:** `arbitrary-origin`
**Severity:** info

**Vulnerable response headers:**
```
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://unq5j.magwien.gv.at
```
The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header reflects an arbitrary origin from the `Origin` header, while `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` is also set. This enables cross-origin data theft when combined with a user who has an active session.

**Note:** The reflected origin `https://unq5j.magwien.gv.at` appears to be another Vienna subdomain — not a completely arbitrary attacker-controlled domain. However, this still permits cross-origin reads of `hyzdok.magwien.gv.at` data from any Vienna subdomain the attacker controls or persuades a user to visit.

---

### 3. CORS Misconfiguration (Info)
**Target:** `https://stadtservicebot.wien.gv.at`
**Port:** 443 | **URL:** `https://stadtservicebot.wien.gv.at`
**Matcher:** `arbitrary-origin`
**Severity:** info

**Vulnerable response headers:**
```
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://vlkkjwien.gv.at
```

Same pattern as above — arbitrary origin reflection with credentials enabled. This is the Stadtservice Wien chatbot portal, and `https://vlkkjwien.gv.at` is the reflected origin.

## Subdomain Inventory (2,083 total)

| Domain | Subdomains |
|--------|-----------|
| wien.gv.at | 1,378 |
| magwien.gv.at | 384 |
| gesundheitsverbund.at | 133 |
| wienkav.at | 188 |

**Notable live services:**
- `www.konto.wien.gv.at` — citizen login portal (Bootstrap/jQuery)
- `www.intern.magwien.gv.at` — internal auth portal
- `www.signatur.wien.gv.at` — IIS/ASP.NET digital signature portal (HSTS)
- `www.wiki.wien.gv.at` — 502 Bad Gateway (Qlik Sense, Element, n8n — interesting stack)
- `ybbs.gesundheitsverbund.at` — WordPress hospital site
- `xml.wien.gv.at` — 404 on XML endpoint (legacy service)
- `hyzdok.magwien.gv.at` — HyzDok document management (CORS misconfig)
- `stadtservicebot.wien.gv.at` — Stadtservice Wien chat widget (CORS misconfig)

---

## Security Observations

- **Widespread HSTS** — most wien.gv.at subdomains enforce HTTPS
- **Strict CSP on many sites** — `default-src 'self'` blocks most XSS vectors
- **Azure Front Door + CDN** — several hosts behind cloud proxies
- **WordPress on hospital sites** — plugin/theme attack surface
- **gau failure** — URL scraping with query params is broken on this system, preventing effective dalfox/sqlmap coverage

---

## Status: COMPLETE (partial coverage)

- ✅ Steps 1, 3, 4 complete
- ⚠️ Steps 2, 5, 6 skipped (gau broken on this system)
- 3 info findings valid and worth reporting

## Recommendation

Report the CORS misconfigs — they're valid and the CORS-with-credentials variant is a meaningful finding even at info severity. The SSRF is worth testing further with a collaborator server to confirm exploitable data exfiltration.
